items.q: If an object is moving at constant velocity in a straight line, what is the net force on it?
items.correct: Zero — by Newton's first law, constant velocity requires zero net force.
items.misconception: There must be a forward force on the object to keep it moving.
items.random_wrong: The net force equals the velocity times the mass of the object.
items.why: This is the classic 'impetus' misconception — everyday experience (objects slow down due to friction) leads students to believe motion requires a continuous force.

items.q: In a collision between two objects, what quantity is always conserved (in a closed system)?
items.correct: Total momentum is always conserved; kinetic energy is only conserved in elastic collisions.
items.misconception: Kinetic energy is always conserved in collisions because energy is conserved.
items.random_wrong: Velocity is always conserved in any collision.
items.why: Students conflate momentum conservation with energy conservation; they overlook that energy can become heat/sound in inelastic collisions.

items.q: In which direction does an electric field point near a positive charge?
items.correct: Radially outwards — the field is defined by the force on a positive test charge, which is repelled.
items.misconception: Radially inwards because positive attracts negative.
items.random_wrong: There is no field around a positive charge unless another charge is present.
items.why: Students confuse the convention (positive test charge) and forget that an isolated positive source still has a field even without another charge.
The answer examiners want is: Rotation/movement. “Sound only” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.correct: A wire through a magnetic field
items.why: Correct answer: A wire through a magnetic field. “Heat through a wire” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: How many components are needed for a simple electromagnet (wire, battery, iron core)?
items.why: Exact answer: 3. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
